sourceTitle: Show your team how to organize, track and manage work in Notion

questions.question: Which of the following best describes a primary benefit of using Notion’s permission settings for a team workspace?

options.text: It automatically creates tasks for every team member.
options.explanation: Permissions control who can view or edit pages; they do not generate tasks.

options.text: It lets you give everyone the same level of access to all pages.
options.explanation: The benefit is the ability to customize access levels, not to give everyone identical rights.

options.text: It allows you to share top‑level OKRs with all while restricting edit rights to senior management.
options.explanation: Correct – permissions let you share view‑only content broadly and limit editing to specific roles.

options.text: It prevents any external collaborators from ever accessing Notion pages.
options.explanation: Notion can share pages with external users when you allow it; permissions do not outright block all external access.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a "teamspace" in Notion?

options.text: To create a private personal dashboard for each employee.
options.explanation: Teamspaces are shared areas for departments, not private personal dashboards.

options.text: To group together pages and databases for a specific department, with its own admin controls.
options.explanation: Correct – teamspaces organize content by department and allow admins to set permissions.

options.text: To replace the need for a company wiki.
options.explanation: A company wiki is typically the top‑level homepage; teamspaces complement it, not replace it.

options.text: To automatically sync data with external tools like Jira.
options.explanation: Syncing with external tools is done via embeds or synced databases, not by teamspace itself.

questions.question: Which three databases are recommended as foundational for a new team workspace?

options.text: Meeting notes, Docs, and Project & Task management
options.explanation: These three are explicitly listed as the key starter databases.

options.text: Employee directory, Expense tracker, and Candidate tracker
options.explanation: While useful, they are not the three core databases recommended for every team.

options.text: OKR tracker, Knowledge base, and Social feed
options.explanation: These are optional extensions, not the primary three suggested.

options.text: Calendar, Timeline, and Kanban board
options.explanation: These are view layouts, not distinct databases.

questions.question: How can relation properties improve workflow visibility across databases?

options.text: They duplicate data so each database has its own copy of information.
options.explanation: Relation properties link records; they do not duplicate data.

options.text: They allow you to connect tasks to projects, showing how many tasks remain for each project.
options.explanation: Correct – relations create connections that surface metrics like remaining tasks per project.

options.text: They automatically assign tasks to the correct team member based on project role.
options.explanation: Relations only link records; assignment still requires manual or formula‑based logic.

options.text: They replace the need for permissions by making all data public.
options.explanation: Relations do not affect permissions; access control remains separate.

questions.question: When customizing your personal task dashboard, which Notion feature lets you view only the tasks you’re tagged in?

options.text: Using a Linked view of the task database with a filter for "Person is [Your Name]".
options.explanation: A linked view with a filter on the person property shows only tasks assigned to you.

options.text: Changing the page permissions to private.
options.explanation: Permissions hide the page but don’t filter tasks by assignee.

options.text: Applying a dark theme to the dashboard.
options.explanation: Theme changes affect appearance only, not content filtering.

options.text: Embedding a Google Sheet with your tasks.
options.explanation: Embedding an external sheet could show tasks but doesn’t use Notion’s built‑in filter capability.

questions.question: What is a key advantage of embedding external tools (e.g., Jira, GitHub) into Notion pages?

options.text: It automatically syncs all changes made in the external tool back to Notion in real time.
options.explanation: Embeds display information but do not provide full bi‑directional sync by default.

options.text: It centralizes information so team members can view updates without leaving Notion.
options.explanation: Embedding lets everyone see external data within Notion, reducing context‑switching.

options.text: It converts the external tool’s data into Notion databases automatically.
options.explanation: Conversion requires manual import or synced databases; embeds alone don’t create Notion tables.

options.text: It removes the need for any permissions on the external tool.
options.explanation: Access to the embedded content still respects the external tool’s own permission settings.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about Notion templates is FALSE?

options.text: Templates can be used to quickly create new records with pre‑filled property values.
options.explanation: This is true; templates speed up record creation.

options.text: Templates are only available for pages, not for database entries.
options.explanation: Incorrect – Notion offers templates for both pages and database entries.

options.text: You can customize a template to include a meeting agenda, project brief, or sprint plan.
options.explanation: True; templates are fully customizable for various use cases.

options.text: Using a template does not affect the underlying database structure.
options.explanation: Correct – templates add content but keep the database schema unchanged.

questions.question: How does an upvote database facilitate cross‑team brainstorming, and what limitation should teams be aware of?

options.text: It lets anyone add ideas and vote, creating a ranked list; however, it cannot enforce anonymity for votes.
options.explanation: Upvote databases surface ideas via voting, but Notion does not currently support anonymous voting, which can affect candid feedback.

options.text: It automatically assigns the most upvoted idea to a project manager; however, it requires a premium plan.
options.explanation: Upvote databases do not auto‑assign tasks, and no plan restriction exists for voting.

options.text: It integrates directly with Slack to post votes in real time; however, it only works for public pages.
options.explanation: While Notion can embed Slack, the upvote feature itself does not push real‑time updates to Slack.

options.text: It provides a visual Kanban board of ideas; however, it cannot filter ideas by department.
options.explanation: An upvote database is a regular database; you can apply filters, so the limitation described is inaccurate.
